data.description:
## Summary
The mkdir leg of the unified write lock (`.ticket-write.lock.d`, `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`) has **no stale-lock reclamation**. The fcntl leg (`.ticket-write.lock`) is kernel-backed and auto-released when the holder dies, but `_acquire_mkdir` only polls `os.mkdir` until the deadline — so a `.ticket-write.lock.d` orphaned by a writer/push that was SIGKILLed mid-commit persists forever and makes **every subsequent write block the full lock budget (~60s) and then fail** with `LockTimeout`.

Discovered while root-causing `slim-fetch-ledge` (rebar show empty-stdout-under-contention). That P1 was fixed on the READ side (the read-path reconverge now waits only ~2s for the lock, commit 9943f1e1), so reads are immune to a stale mkdir lock. WRITES remain affected and are tracked here separately.

## Reproduction Steps
1. In an initialized store, simulate an orphaned lock: `mkdir .tickets-tracker/.ticket-write.lock.d` (no live holder).
2. Run any write, e.g. `rebar comment <id> "x"`.
3. Observe it hangs ~60s (the write lock budget = timeout x attempts) then fails with `LockTimeout`, even though no process holds the lock.

- **Expected:** a provably-stale mkdir lock (no live owner) is reclaimed so writes proceed promptly.
- **Actual:** the orphaned dir blocks every write for the full budget, then the write fails.

## Notes on a safe fix
- The fcntl leg is the real mutual-exclusion primitive (acquired first in `acquire`). The mkdir leg is a secondary dual-window/bash-interop guard.
- Reclamation must be conservative — incorrectly removing a live lock breaks mutual exclusion and can corrupt the store. Options: record the owner PID (and host/boot id) inside the lock dir on acquire and reclaim only when that PID is provably dead; and/or gate reclamation on the fact that the caller already holds the fcntl leg (safe only once the bash write core / dual_window is fully retired — verify before relying on it).
- macOS lacks util-linux `flock(1)`, so historically bash could hold ONLY the mkdir leg there ("stiff-mop-lane" gap); confirm the bash write core is fully retired before treating "we hold fcntl" as proof the mkdir dir is stale.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A provably-stale `.ticket-write.lock.d` (no live owning process) is reclaimed so a write proceeds without waiting the full lock budget.
- [ ] A LIVE lock holder is never reclaimed — mutual exclusion is preserved (concurrency test under contention).
- [ ] A regression test: with an orphaned mkdir lock (dead/absent owner), a write completes promptly instead of hanging ~60s then failing.
- [ ] Reclamation is documented in `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` alongside the dual-window rule.
data.title: mkdir write-lock leg (.ticket-write.lock.d) has no stale-lock reclamation; orphaned dir blocks all writes
